title: Advanced LLMs
subtitle: Reasoning, Agents, and the Structure of Intelligence
description: An advanced course that will dig deepet into the current trends in LLM development including reasoning and agents, and provide better understanding of the strengths and limitations of current AI.

instructor.name: Dr. Amir Sadovnik
instructor.bio: Dr. Sadovnik is the Research Lead for the newly established Center for AI Security Research (CAISER) at Oak Ridge National Lab. As part of this role, Dr. Sadovnik leads multiple research projects related to AI risk, adversarial AI, and large language model vulnerabilities.

learningOutcomes: Gain a deeper understanding of how LLMs operate including their strengths, limitations, and security risks.
Gain competence in using LLMs with common frameworks both locally and on the cloud
Understand the basics of LLM reasoning, hallucinations, and interpretability.
Understand and design LLM based autonomous agentic systems and using common frameworks.
Implement and benchmark LLMs on a novel task and right a academic level paper on you resutls.

policies.submission: All work must be submitted via Canvas. These will usually be by 11:59 PM EST on the due date. Late submissions will not be accepted. While some assignments will be independent others will be done in groups
policies.honesty: Students are allowed to use LLMs (ChatGPT, Claude, etc.) as coding assistants, but must disclose their usage. Also, students must make sure they understand all code they have written. Plagiarism of academic papers or other students' work will result in immediate failure.
policies.attendance: This is a seminar-style course. Active participation is required. Attendance is expected. Please read all assigned papers *before* class.
policies.additional: Please make sure to find out the last day to drop/withdraw from the course if needed. For campus-wide policies on disability accommodations and mental health resources, please visit https://teaching.utk.edu/wp-content/uploads/sites/78/2026/01/Campus-Syllabus-Updated-1-8-26.2.docx

assignments.title: Quizes/Exam
assignments.desc: Every few classes we will start with a few questions that'll cover last week's class. We will probably have a longer one later in the semester as well.

assignments.title: Final Project
assignments.desc: Novel research implementation or reproduction of a SOTA result. Groups of 3-4. Requires quantitative evaluation metrics.

assignments.title: Homeworks
assignments.desc: On time and correct submission of assignments. All assignments will be posted on Canvas.

weeks.desc: Understanding the basics of artificial neural networks - the backbone of LLMS - including basic math.

readings.title: The Math Behind Neural Networks
readings.author: Cristian Leo (2024)
readings.note: A website with a brief overview of neural networks

weeks.desc: A deeper dive into the transformer architecture and basics of LLM's.

readings.title: The Illustrated Transformer
readings.author: Jay Alammar (2018)
readings.note: A website with a good description of transformers

readings.title: A Beginner’s Guide to Tokens, Vectors, and Embeddings in NLP
readings.author: Sascha Metzger (2022)
readings.note: A website with a overview of tokenization and embedding

weeks.desc: Moving beyond perplexity. How do we measure intelligence without contamination.

readings.title: HarmBench: A Standardized Evaluation Framework for Automated Red Teaming and Robust Refusal
readings.author: Hendrycks et al. (2024)

readings.title: Holistic Evaluation of Language Models (HELM) pages 1-10
readings.author: Liang et al. (2022)

readings.title: An Empirical Study of LLM-as-a-Judge: How Design Choices Impact Evaluation Reliability
readings.author: Yamauchi et al. (2025)

weeks.desc: Eliciting reasoning through intermediate computation steps.
Non-linear exploration of solution spaces: Trees and Graphs.

readings.title: Tree of Thoughts: Deliberate Problem Solving
readings.author: Yao et al. (2023)

readings.title: Graph of Thoughts: Solving Elaborate Problems
readings.author: Besta et al. (2023)

readings.title: DeepSeek-R1: Incentivizing Reasoning Capability in LLMs via Reinforcement Learning
readings.author: DeepSeek-AI (2005)

weeks.desc: Can models fix their own mistakes? The limits of verification.

readings.title: Large Language Models Cannot Self-Correct Reasoning Yet
readings.author: Valmeekam et al. (2023)

weeks.desc: Critical analysis: Pattern matching vs. true logic.

readings.title: Faith and Fate: Limits on Compositionality
readings.author: Dziri et al. (2023)

readings.title: Toolformer: Models Can Teach Themselves Tools
readings.author: Schick et al. (2023)

weeks.desc: Moving to a different agnetic structure: planning and execution

readings.title: Plan-and-Solve Prompting: Improving Zero-Shot Chain-of-Thought Reasoning by Large Language Models
readings.author: Wang et al. (2023)

readings.title: An LLM Compiler for Parallel Function Calling
readings.author: Kim et al. (2023)

readings.title: ReWOO: Decoupling Reasoning from Observations for Efficient Augmented Language Models
readings.author: Xu er al. (2023)

readings.title: Plan-and-Act: Improving Planning of Agents for Long-Horizon Tasks
readings.author: Erdogan er al. (2025)

weeks.desc: Persisting state over time. The 'Smallville' architecture.

readings.title: Generative Agents: Simulacra of Human Behavior
readings.author: Park et al. (2023)

weeks.desc: Social behavior and collaboration between synthetic minds.

readings.title: ChatDev: Communicative Agents for Software Dev
readings.author: Qian et al. (2023)

weeks.desc: Mapping the neural circuitry and steering behavior.
Hackathon week. Focus on 'The Engineering of Reliability'.
